Sectoral Drivers of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Growing Role of Wildfires¶

Presented as a California Wildfire Case Study¶

Angela Larson¶

Project page: https://angelalarson.github.io/angelalarson.github.io-main/project/project.html

Github page: https://github.com/angelalarson/angelalarson.github.io-main

Project Goal:

This project analyzes greenhouse gas emissions across U.S. states and territories using historical emissions inventories and wildfire emissions data. First, I aim to explore long-term trends in emissions across states between 1990 and 2022. Using time-series analysis, I will compare changes in total emissions over time and compare trends across emission sectors such as transportation, energy, and agriculture. I will also identify which sectors drive emissions in each state then use this to predict future state emissions. These analyses will provide a baseline understanding of the major drivers of greenhouse gas emissions across the United States.

Then, I want to specifically observe California state emissions and wildfire emissions. Despite wildfire emissions being included in each state greenhouse gas inventory, the use of a separate wildfire dataset provides event-level information such as acres burned and emissions per fire. I will compare wildfire emissions against other major emission sectors in California to better understand their relative contribution to total emissions. I intend to use a regression model to predict total California emissions using historical anthropogenic sector emissions and wildfire activity indicators. By comparing model predictions using anthropogenic emissions alone versus models that incorporate wildfire emissions and fire activity metrics, I aim to demonstrate how wildfire activity is increasingly contributing to California greenhouse gas emissions variability. Additionally, I aim to answer if the 2020-2021 combined drought and COVID-19 events in California shift the relative contribution of wildfire emissions compared to traditionally dominant industrial sectors.

Description of Datasets:

The first dataset I will be using includes individual state and U.S. territory greenhouse gas emissions, grouped by greenhouse gas (Tg) and economic sector. These data range from years 1990-2022. Each row represents one greenhouse gas emission per year includes economic sector and subsectors, UNFCCC-IPCC sector and subsectors, carbon pool, fuel type, greenhouse gas type, and emission per year. This extensive dataset uses governmental language and is meant to inform emissions policies.

State greenhouse gas emissions data: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) State Greenhouse Gas Inventory Dataset can be downloaded through the zip file under "Download state-level GHG data" section. https://www.epa.gov/ghgemissions/methodology-report-inventory-us-greenhouse-gas-emissions-and-sinks-state-1990-2022

The second dataset I will be using is California wildfire emissions data between the years of 2015-2024, compilied by the California Air Resources Board (CARB). Each row represents one wildfire event, represented by a unique wildfire ID and presents data on the number of acres burned, emissions, and fuel consumption data in addition to emissions per individual greenhouse gas (tons). These data are meant to support public knowledge of wildfire emissions and air quality risk.

California wildfire emissions data: California Air Resources Board (CARB) Wildfire Emissions Inventory Dataset can be downloaded by clicking on "Summary Table" under "Wildfire Emissions Data" section. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/wildfire-emissions

One question I aim to answer is how wildfire emission trends and industry emission trends differ. To do so, I will observe emissions trends in 2020/21, where production emissions will likely be decreased due to the COVID-19 pandemic but wildfire emissions will likely be increased due to particularly tragic drought years in California through time series analysis.

While the all-state greenhouse gas emissions dataset includes a large breadth of data, the wildfire dataset is limited by its lack of national relevancy as who produced this dataset is the California-specific government sector California Air Resources Board. There is no standardized wildfire dataset to be found for each individual state, nor does this dataset exceed ten years worth of data. This has limited the depth of this data analysis and reproducibility per state.

In [ ]:
# import required packages
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# modeling
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, r2_score
from sklearn.feature_extraction import DictVectorizer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_validate
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsRegressor

# load wildfire emissions dataset
fires = pd.read_csv("Wildfire-Veg_EmissInv_2015-2024_Summary.csv")

# display beginning rows
display(ghg.head())
display(fires.head())

In [ ]:
# clean greenhouse gas emission data
# find all year columns
year_cols = [c for c in ghg.columns if c.startswith('Y')]

# tidy data
ghg_long = ghg.melt(
    id_vars=[
        'geo_ref',
        'ghg',
        'ghg_category',
        'sector',
        'subsector',
        'category',
        'sub_category_1',
        'fuel1',
        'fuel2'
    ],
    value_vars=year_cols,
    var_name='year',
    value_name='emissions_Tg'
)

# clean year
ghg_long['year'] = ghg_long['year'].str.replace('Y','').astype(int)

# convert 0 emissions to NaN
ghg_long['emissions_Tg'] = ghg_long['emissions_Tg'].replace(0, np.nan)

# filter for CO2 only
ghg_long = ghg_long[ghg_long['ghg'] == 'Carbon Dioxide']

# subset California (this will be useful later...)
ca_ghg = ghg_long[ghg_long['geo_ref'] == 'CA']

display(ghg_long.head())
display(ghg_long.dtypes)
display(ca_ghg.head())

# highest emission states ranked
state_year = ghg_long.groupby(
    ['geo_ref','year']
)['emissions_Tg'].sum().reset_index()

# group dataset by state, select emissions and greenhouse gas, and sort the values from most to least
state_rank = state_year.groupby('geo_ref')['emissions_Tg'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

# display beginning rows
state_rank.head()

By ranking the states by average CO2 emissions between 1992 to 2022, I gain a better understanding of the biggest emitters in the country. This provides me with context for when completing future analyis in conjunction with the wildfire dataset to the state specific contributions and relative differences between them. Texas is number one, just above California (CA). Given the larger population and combustion of CA natural carbon stock, I wouldn't have expected this; however, Texas has a large oil and gas industry so the large emissions can likely be attributed to this.

In [ ]:
# emission change since 1990 per state
emissions_1990 = state_year[state_year['year'] == 1990]
emissions_recent = state_year[state_year['year'] == 2022]

# calculate change in emissions
delta_emission = emissions_recent.set_index('geo_ref')['emissions_Tg'] - emissions_1990.set_index('geo_ref')['emissions_Tg']

# rank largest change in emissions descending
display(delta_emission.sort_values(ascending=False).head(10))

Initial exploration of this dataset includes change in emissions between 1990 and 2022 for time series analysis. The states and territories that show decreases in emissions could reflect changes in policy or positive land use that increased carbon sinks, but more likely reflect a lack of emission accounting in earlier years. This provides me with context on how to better handle the data (ie, accounting for NaN values and only including specific years where all states are reflected).

This provides great context as to which sectors result in the highest carbon dioxide emissions across the broader U.S. and compels me to look closer at certain sectors such as 'Land Use, Land-Use Change and Forestry' as it is negative. # plot the sector counts
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

bars = plt.barh(sector_counts.index, sector_counts.values)

plt.xlabel("Number of States")
plt.ylabel("Sector")

# add value labels
for bar in bars:
    width = bar.get_width()
    plt.text(width + 0.2, # arrange number label location
             bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
             int(width),
             va='center')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This visualization is useful for exploring where the majority of carbon dioxide emissions lie within each state and territory. We see an abundance of energy-dominated emission states and territories, informing an anthropogenic baseline of sorts as to what are emission drivers across the country.

In [ ]:
# now clean wildfire data
# fix column names
fires.columns = fires.columns.str.replace('\n', '_').str.strip().str.lower()

# year must be numeric
fires['year'] = fires['year'].astype(int)

# aggregate wildfire data by year
fires_yearly = fires.groupby('year').agg({
    'acres_burned': 'sum',
    'co2_(tons)': 'sum'
}).reset_index()

# rename
fires_yearly.rename(columns={
    'co2_(tons)': 'wildfire_co2_ton'
}, inplace=True)

# convert tons to teragrams and drop extra column
fires_yearly['wildfire_co2_tg'] = fires_yearly['wildfire_co2_ton'] * 1e-6
fires_yearly.drop(columns=['wildfire_co2_ton'], inplace=True)

display(fires_yearly)
display(fires_yearly.dtypes)
year acres_burned wildfire_co2_tg

In [ ]:
# combine data sets by adding wildfire as a sector and applying only the valid years (where wildfire years = ghg years)
# get valid years
valid_years = ca_ghg['year'].unique()

# keep only wildfire years that exist in ca_ghg
fires_yearly = fires_yearly[fires_yearly['year'].isin(valid_years)]

# add sector label
fires_yearly['sector'] = 'Wildfire'

# rename emissions column
fires_yearly.rename(columns={'wildfire_co2_tg': 'emissions_Tg'}, inplace=True)

# keep only needed columns
fires_yearly = fires_yearly[['year', 'sector', 'emissions_Tg']]

# keep only matching columns
ca_ghg_subset = ca_ghg[['year', 'sector', 'emissions_Tg']]

#combine datasets
ca_combined = pd.concat([ca_ghg_subset, fires_yearly], ignore_index=True)


# aggregate to ensure one value per year-sector pair
ca_combined = (
    ca_combined
    .groupby(['year', 'sector'])['emissions_Tg']
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
)

display(ca_combined)
display(ca_combined.dtypes)

# check for missing values
ca_combined.isna().sum()

# check year coverage
print("Year range:", ca_combined['year'].min(), "-", ca_combined['year'].max())

# check sectors
print(ca_combined['sector'].unique())

In [ ]:
# create the total aggregation (your current code)
ca_total = ca_combined.groupby('year')['emissions_Tg'].sum().reset_index()

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 8))

#plot individual sectors using the long-format ca_combined
# We use a lower alpha (transparency) so they don't drown out the total
sns.lineplot(data=ca_combined, x='year', y='emissions_Tg', hue='sector',
             linewidth=1.5, marker='o', alpha=0.7)

# plot the total emissinos line on top
plt.plot(ca_total['year'], ca_total['emissions_Tg'], color='black',
         linewidth=4, label='Total California Emissions', linestyle='-')

plt.title("California Emissions: Sector Breakdown vs. Total (1990-2022)", fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel("Year", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("CO2 Emissions (Tg)", fontsize=12)
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This graph is a great representation of how California emissions are so greatly affected by energy usage, but when the variability of the wildfire dataset is introduced in 2015, it causes total California emissions to vary greatly from the energy trend.

In [ ]:
# filter for overlap period
overlap_mask = (ca_combined['year'] >= 2015) & (ca_combined['year'] <= 2022)
ca_overlap = ca_combined[overlap_mask]

# calculate total for this time and plot sectors
ca_total_overlap = ca_overlap.groupby('year')['emissions_Tg'].sum().reset_index()
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 8))
sns.lineplot(data=ca_overlap, x='year', y='emissions_Tg', hue='sector',
             linewidth=2, marker='o', alpha=0.8)

# plot total emissions
plt.plot(ca_total_overlap['year'], ca_total_overlap['emissions_Tg'], color='black',
         linewidth=5, label='Total California Emissions', linestyle='-', alpha=0.9)

# formatting
plt.title("California Emissions by Sector (2015-2022)", fontsize=18, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel("Year", fontsize=14)
plt.ylabel("CO2 Emissions (Tg)", fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(range(2015, 2023)) # Ensure every year shows up
plt.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left', fontsize=12)

# highlight covid years
plt.axvspan(2020, 2021, color='yellow', alpha=0.1, label='COVID/Drought Peak')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Zooming in on just 2015-2022, where the sectors dataset overlaps with the wildfire dataset, we can see how greatly the effect of wildfires is felt during the COVID-19 pandemic (highlighted in yellow), when less energy was used and drought was high.

In [ ]:
# time for the model!
# pivot sectors into features (X) and sum them for the target (y)
df_final = ca_combined[overlap_mask].pivot(index='year', columns='sector', values='emissions_Tg').fillna(0)
X_dict = df_final.to_dict(orient="records")
y = df_final.sum(axis=1)

# initializing tools
vec = DictVectorizer(sparse=False)
scaler = StandardScaler()

# hyperparameter tuning
def get_cv_error(k):
    model = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=k)
    pipeline = Pipeline([("vectorizer", vec), ("scaler", scaler), ("fit", model)])
    # We use cv=3 due to the small sample size (n=8)
    mse = np.mean(-cross_validate(
        pipeline, X_dict, y,
        cv=KFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),
        scoring="neg_mean_squared_error"
    )["test_score"])
    return mse

# test k values from 1 to 5
ks = pd.Series(range(1, 6), index=range(1, 6))
test_errs = ks.apply(get_cv_error)

# plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
test_errs.plot.line(marker='o', color='darkorange')
plt.title("Model Tuning: MSE vs. k Neighbors")
plt.xlabel("k")
plt.ylabel("Mean Squared Error")
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

best_k = test_errs.idxmin()
print(f"Optimal k discovered: {best_k}")

Optimal k discovered: 1

A 1-NN model is not great and subject to overfitting. This is very likely the result of a small dataset, which is why is becomes prone to overfitting.

In [ ]:
# beta/effect size calculations
# use linear regression to calculate effect
lr_model = LinearRegression()
lr_pipeline = Pipeline([("vectorizer", vec), ("scaler", scaler), ("fit", lr_model)])
lr_pipeline.fit(X_dict, y)

# extract coefficients from the 'fit' step of the pipeline
# standardize the data
betas = lr_pipeline.named_steps['fit'].coef_
feature_names = lr_pipeline.named_steps['vectorizer'].get_feature_names_out()

effect_sizes = pd.Series(betas, index=feature_names).sort_values(ascending=False)

# plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
effect_sizes.plot(kind='barh', color='royalblue')
plt.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=1)
plt.title("Inference: Standardized Effect Sizes (Beta) by Sector")
plt.xlabel("Effect Size (Change in Total Emissions per Std Dev)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

While all data is measured in Teragrams, I standardized the features to compare the relative influence of each sector. This allowed me to see which sector’s fluctuations, more than just total volume, are most responsible for the changes in California's total emissions.

In [ ]:
# plot actual vs predicted to assess the model
knn_final = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=best_k)
knn_pipeline = Pipeline([("vectorizer", vec), ("scaler", scaler), ("fit", knn_final)])

# plot
knn_pipeline.fit(X_dict, y)
preds = knn_pipeline.predict(X_dict)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
plt.scatter(y, preds, color='crimson', s=100, label=f'k-NN (k={best_k}) Predictions')
plt.plot([y.min(), y.max()], [y.min(), y.max()], 'k--', label="Perfect Prediction")
plt.title("3-NN Model Performance: Actual vs. Predicted")
plt.xlabel("Actual Total Emissions (Tg)")
plt.ylabel("Predicted Total Emissions (Tg)")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

Just as expected, overfitting occured with the 1-NN model. Based on the MSE vs K-NN figure, we see the next best option is a 3-NN model.

In [ ]:
# setup the 3-NN pipeline
vec = DictVectorizer(sparse=False)
scaler = StandardScaler()
model_3nn = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=3)

pipeline_3nn = Pipeline([
    ("vectorizer", vec),
    ("scaler", scaler),
    ("fit", model_3nn)
])

# 3-fold cross-validation
cv_results_3nn = cross_validate(
    pipeline_3nn, X_dict, y,
    cv=KFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),
    scoring="neg_mean_squared_error",
    return_train_score=True
)

# calc MSE
train_mse_3nn = -cv_results_3nn["train_score"].mean()
test_mse_3nn = -cv_results_3nn["test_score"].mean()

print(f"--- 3-NN Model Results ---")
print(f"Training MSE: {train_mse_3nn:.4f}")
print(f"Validation (Test) MSE: {test_mse_3nn:.4f}")

# plot
pipeline_3nn.fit(X_dict, y)
preds_3nn = pipeline_3nn.predict(X_dict)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.scatter(y, preds_3nn, color='darkviolet', s=120, label='3-NN Predictions', alpha=0.8)
plt.plot([y.min(), y.max()], [y.min(), y.max()], 'k--', label='Perfect Fit Line')

plt.title("Model Demonstration: 3-Nearest Neighbors Performance")
plt.xlabel("Actual Emissions (Tg)")
plt.ylabel("Predicted Emissions (Tg)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.2)
plt.show()

--- 3-NN Model Results ---
Training MSE: 282.4226
Validation (Test) MSE: 443.2289

By using k=3, I reduce the impact of outliers. If one year had a massive wildfire spike (such as 2020 and 2021), a 1-NN model would predict a spike for any year that looks even slightly similar with other sector emissions. The 3-NN model will look at other surrounding data points first. Additionally, moving from k=1 to k=3 will reduce variance even if it slightly increases the distance between points as it will mitigate overfitting. Still not perfect, but better than an overfit model.

I would really appreciate some feedback on how I went about the model and questions to answer. I was also considering trying to predict change in sector effect size over time, but I was struggling with how best to do this since the dataset is so limited in length!
